data.title: Bigger Has Not Been Better For Glacier Bancorp
data.description:
This article serves as an update to my previous analysis for Glacier Bancorp, Inc. (GBCI), which was published on Seeking Alpha on October 11. At that time, I rated its stock as a Hold based on valuation concerns as well as a lack of a real catalyst to move its price forward. Since that first article was published, GBCI has a total return of -3.83%, just a couple of percentage points behind the S&P 500 but lagging the State Street SPDR S&P Regional Banking ETF (KRE) by almost 10%.

Last June, Glacier Bancorp announced the acquisition of Guaranty Bancshares. The deal closed at the end of Q3 2025, marking GBCI’s 13th banking deal in the last decade. While the pace of deal-making makes it more challenging to fully appreciate the impact of each transaction individually, the Guaranty merger allowed Glacier to have greater access to attractive markets in the state of Texas. Unlike other regional banks that acquire smaller competitors and fold their operations under the name of the larger bank, Glacier uses a different model, allowing acquired companies to keep their names while their branches operate under one technology platform. Glacier Bancorp is now the holding company for 18 different banks, as shown in the slide below.

I do like the advantages that this model presents for smaller banks. Maintaining local brand names can give a branch a much more “community feel” and help prevent some bank customers from shopping around their accounts as they might if their bank changed brands. It also keeps customers from feeling as if they are being pushed into a larger banking system, even though Glacier now has a market cap of $5.7 billion.

GBCI is set to release its earnings for Q1 2026 on April 23. Based on the results from last year and the information the company shared in its investor presentation from February of this year, I still do not see a rush to create a new position in its stock. In this analysis, I will provide support for maintaining my Hold rating on Glacier Bancorp pending meaningful improvements to its operations and its bottom line in the year ahead.

Company Overview

Glacier Bancorp is the holding company for a collection of regional and community banks that have a geographic footprint that touches nine states from Texas to Washington. When looking at the loan balances from its branch locations, the largest shares of the company’s assets are found in Montana (28%), Idaho (14%), and Utah (13%). The Guaranty deal quickly moved Texas to fourth on that list at 10%. Further details about the company can be seen below, including its total assets, gross loans, and total deposits as of December 31, 2025.

Additional details about the company’s balance sheet data dating back over the last five years can be seen in the table below. Glacier provided this information as part of its 2025 10-K. While growth in several areas last year looks impressive, much of those increases can be attributed to the addition of Guaranty Bancshares. Over the last five years, the compound average growth rate for loans at the company is a healthy 9.3%, but total assets have only expanded 4.3% per year during this time as the company’s debt securities portfolio has decreased significantly.

Glacier Bancorp’s loan portfolio at the end of last year was heavily dependent on commercial real estate. CRE comprised 64.8% of the total loans receivable. The next closest loan segment was other commercial loans, which had an allocation of 16.7%. Residential real estate was third in weight at 11.7%, the only other loan segment above 5% for GBCI.

Glacier Bancorp pays a quarterly dividend of $0.33 per share and has paid out that same amount since its first regular distribution in 2022. As I noted in my first analysis, the payout ratio for GBCI is elevated, standing at 66.0% at the end of 2025. This decreases the likelihood that a dividend increase will be forthcoming for shareholders anytime soon. The stock’s forward yield of 3.02% is just six basis points behind the median for the financial sector.

Analysis Of Glacier Bancorp

Glacier Bancorp released its Q4 2025 and FY 2025 financial results back on January 22. This was the first full quarter that the company was able to show the results of its transaction for Guaranty Bancshares. Diluted earnings per share for Q4 were $0.49, a decrease of $0.08 per share, or 14%, from Q3. It was also a decline of $0.05, or 9%, from Q4 2024. Diluted earnings per share of $1.99 for FY 2025 were 18.5% better than FY 2024 but 1% worse than FY 2023.

The company’s performance on its bottom line has been disappointing for an extended period of time. It is noticeable that FY 2025 was the first year that Glacier has been able to show earnings growth since all the way back in FY 2021. The table below shows the performance of the bank during the last five years. The key to the turnaround for the company last year was its ability to grow interest income while decreasing interest expense.

If there is one area where the company has really made the most noticeable improvement in the last year, it would be net interest margin. After ending 2024 below 3%, NIM reached 3.58% in Q4 2025. The improvement of 61 basis points in NIM allowed Glacier to boost net interest income by $75 million, or 39%, when compared to Q4 of the previous year.

The remainder of the performance metrics for GBCI paint a mixed picture. Return on assets in Q4 2025 stood at 0.78%, a decline of 15 basis points from Q3 2025 and nine basis points from Q4 2024. Return on average equity was just 6.59% for FY 2025. This was an improvement from 2024’s final result of 6.02%, but it was also a far cry from 2022 and 2021, when the company’s ROE was above 10% in each year.

Asset quality for the combined performance from GBCI’s 18 banking brands appeared to be in decent shape at the end of 2025, even though the company recorded a sizeable increase in its provision for credit losses as the result of its merger activity. Net charge-offs of 0.06% in FY 2025 were two basis points lower than in FY 2024. The company’s ratio of non-performing assets did show a steady increase in FY 2025, as shown in the graph on the bottom right below, but the ratio is still lower than Glacier’s peers.

Even though GBCI has dropped about 15% since almost reaching $54 per share in early February, it still looks rather expensive. The stock trades at a trailing P/E of almost 22 and a forward P/E of over 14. The most recent tangible book value per share for the company is $21.01. At its current market price of $44.67 per share, GBCI currently trades at 2.13 times its TBV. The median price to TBV for a regional bank at the end of last year was about 1.4.

Risks To Consider

The Q1 2026 financial results from almost all regional banks are expected to show at least some of the impact of the recent uncertainty in the markets related to the conflict in the Middle East. Higher gas prices may have a meaningful impact on consumer spending and consumer savings. Higher long-term interest rates may impact businesses' ability to borrow and expand. The odds of the Federal Reserve cutting rates in the first half of this year have almost moved to zero. With no certain end in sight for most of these issues, their impacts may linger well into Q2 2026 as well. A downturn in the economy could increase nonperforming assets and cause a need to increase provisions for future losses. This is an issue for all regional banks and has been reflected in the declines seen in the State Street SPDR S&P Regional Banking ETF since the end of the first week in February.

Glacier Bancorp actually closed two acquisitions in 2025, with the first being the Bank of Idaho back in May. While multiple deals closed in the same calendar year may eventually have meaningful long-term benefits for the company’s bottom line, there are integration risks that will be overhanging at least the first two quarters of this year. Complications with system conversions, employee turnover, and a chance that expected cost savings will not be achieved are all possible challenges for the company in 2026.

There is also a chance that the company will initiate yet another deal this year, complicating matters further. As part of its Investor Presentation from February, Glacier was not shy about identifying potential targets in both the Mountain West and Southwest regions, as shown in the slide below. The company pointed out that population growth in the Mountain West is expected to be about 3.6% higher than the national average over the next five years. In the Southwest, population growth is projected to be 5.3% greater than the US average of just 0.3%.

All of this M&A activity is having an impact on the company’s balance sheet, as goodwill increased 31.1% in the last 12 months to $1.38 billion as of December 31, 2025. The total amount of goodwill on the books for GBCI is now 32.7% of its total stockholders’ equity. If goodwill that is recorded with Glacier’s previous or future transactions becomes impaired, the company could face accounting charges that impact both its earnings and capital position.

The Bottom Line

As it did back in October, Seeking Alpha's quant rating deems GBCI to be a Hold, although its overall score put it in the Sell category at the start of this year. The stock does receive an above-average score in growth, partially due to the revenue expansion that is expected for FY 2026. The below-average performance in ROA and ROE is a big reason why the stock receives a “C-” in profitability.

Without a compelling reason to believe the stock will outperform its regional bank peers this year, I am maintaining the Hold recommendation that I stated last fall. If the company's asset quality metrics were lower, I could even see justification for a sell rating here. The valuation for GBCI is just not compelling given its rather average performance over the last few years. It will be interesting to see if the company can resist the temptation in the first half of 2026 to become active in the M&A space once again so that investors may have greater visibility on the full impact of its most recent transactions.

data.title: Netflix: Focus On Content, Focus On Its Own
data.description:
At the start of March, I concluded that Netflix (NFLX) was moving ahead on its own after it declined to raise its offer for Warner Bros., thereby effectively pulling out of the bidding war for the business.

I liked the financial discipline displayed, and despite some distractions, Netflix was able to obtain a nice multi-billion termination fee, while it left its peer Paramount Skydance (PSKY) with much more leverage. This, given the recent events (including bond markets), might actually provide an opportunity for Netflix.

That is clearly seen in the share price of Paramount, which has fallen from levels around $13 early in March to lows of $9 today. The weakening of such a competitor, facing a huge debt load in uncertain macro times, is of course a positive for Netflix here. This gives the company more relative power to invest in content or perhaps acquire assets down the road at less competitive prices.

Netflix continues on its own, and following the company quickly returning to focus on internal content, I like the strategic route walked here.

Other, higher conviction ideas, including recent M&A efforts, can be found at Value In Corporate Events.

Going After It Alone

After missing out on Warner Bros., after initially offering $82 billion to acquire the firm, Netflix pulled out of the bidding war for Warner Bros. about a month ago. Co-CEOs Sarandos and Peters stressed financial discipline, quickly pointing out $20 billion in content spending on series and films in 2026. This is designed to create superior competitive offerings and continue to take market share organically, instead of acquiring an iconic studio with all the pricing, integration, and leverage risks associated.

Concerns about such dealmaking and the prospects of a bidding war made that a $130 stock fell to the $100 mark in December and the $80s earlier this year. Shares have now recovered to levels around $95 per share, which is after news about Netflix pulling back broke. Investors were fearful about large M&A, assumption of debt, and different corporate cultures to be integrated, let alone regulatory risk out here.

Meanwhile, Netflix reported a solid 18% increase in fourth-quarter sales to $12.1 billion, with the company ending the year with 375 million subscribers. The company guided for growth to slow down to 15% in the first quarter of 2026, still a solid achievement. Full-year sales were reported at $45 billion, including a $1.5 billion advertising business, while 2026 sales were seen up 12-14% to a midpoint of $51.2 billion.

With operating margins seen around 30.5%, plus or minus a percent, all this created a solid roadmap for operating earnings to come in at $16.1 billion. After applying taxes, earnings could be seen around $3 per share, translating into a 32 times earnings multiple for a largely unleveraged business. Just over $5 billion in net debt is very manageable, set to come down following receipt of a $2.8 billion termination fee.

I like that the business was going after its future alone, as it has done very successfully in the past. However, outside all the opportunities and challenges ahead, there were other risks, such as the rise of AI. Of course, this could help in reducing the price of content; it could be a real concern as well. Given all that, Netflix has gotten a bit more compelling, but that is not the same as seeing great appeal just yet.

Upping The Content Game

To bolster the content offerings, Netflix was not sitting still and quickly moved to make some content announcements. By the end of March, Netflix was reportedly looking to double coverage of National Football League games, this, however, just being a 4-package game.

The key in this is, of course, the rise of sports and live content, both of which are fairly rare in the case of the content library of Netflix. A couple of such deals quickly result in Netflix hitting the $20 billion mark, after it spent $17.1 billion in addition to content assets in 2025. In all fairness, the company is depreciating such content at nearly the same pace here, as discussions about capex and amortization schemes were quite lively in the past.

To pay for such content, Netflix is reportedly looking to raise prices as well, with most of such recent price hikes dating back to the start of 2025. This is likely going to be a key driver behind the anticipated revenue growth this year.

Somewhat ironic and perhaps painful is that Netflix actually teamed up with Warner Music to collaborate on movies and documentaries of renowned musicians and songwriters. Of course, Warner Music no longer has ties to Warner Bros., unlike in the past.

And Now?

Given all these trends, Netflix has quickly found itself in calmer waters. Following the decisive move to pull out of the bidding war, the focus has quickly and decisively shifted to the company's own operations. This results in new content deals, more diversified content deals, which look like the way to go to drive quality and diversified appeal.

This makes me upbeat from an operational and strategic perspective, as a low-thirty times earnings multiple marks a premium. It comes amidst limited leverage, strong growth, and undisputed leadership in the category for attention and entertainment, as the value of Netflix given the time spent is relatively modest. Furthermore, compared to alternatives like television and cable, the pricing of subscriptions still sounds very reasonable in my view.

This could drive continued growth this year, as many have real concerns about the uncertainty of AI, which, of course, could be helpful, but I fear the potential negative impact here. In the bull case, it could help reduce the cost of content creation, but the negatives are potentially much more severe. These have the potential to unleash a wide range of cheaper competitors, at least in terms of content creation, which of course is not the same as distribution.

It is just that concern that prevents me from pulling the trigger, yet any continuation of growth and pullback to the lower $80s could be used as a buying opportunity here.

data.title: Nike Q3: Turnaround Plan Turns Investors Away, Shares Attractive
data.description:
Shares in sneaker and apparel company Nike (NKE) have been taking a drumming of late. The release of its Q3 results only added further pressure to the underperformance.

At current levels, I view this as an attractive opportunity for long-term positioning in a company whose turnaround hasn’t necessarily materialized just yet but is bound to happen in time.

In reporting its Q3, NKE said that it's expecting sales to decline through the end of 2026, with a 2% to 4% decline in its fiscal Q4. That’s a significant miss for about 2% growth.

With U.S. markets already on edge over the ongoing conflict in the Middle East and the associated spike in oil prices, it’s perhaps not a surprise that weary investors were taking no chances with what some could perhaps call a ‘falling knife’ stock.

I, on the other hand, see the double-digit declines as an attractive opportunity to initiate new positioning or to add to an existing one.

NKE Stock Key Metrics

Shares in NKE were already down about 20% over the past year prior to the release of its Q3. Add on another approximately 10% decline post-earnings, and you have NKE trading at historically low pricing.

While the Seeking Alpha (“SA”) quants have never really graded NKE favorably on valuation, I have begged to differ, seeing its price-to-sales ratio as a key indicator of its valuation.

While NKE has certainly struggled with its brand in relation to rising brands, such as HOKA, which is owned by Deckers Outdoor (DECK), NKE is still a global market leader in footwear, and I don’t see that changing anytime soon.

For a market leader such as NKE, I see a sub-1.5x price/sales ratio as overly pessimistic, especially when a competitor such as DECK is trading at nearly double that valuation.

Analysts across SA are also similarly neutral or pessimistic on the stock.

The most recent coverage just before the Q3 release was actually quite timely, and the call is commendable. However, NKE has bounced back before after trading at lows, and I believe it can do so again.

NKE Q3 Results Recap

In my view, it would be a mistake to focus too much on NKE’s headline numbers for Q3. I believe what is more important is what the management team is deliberately doing beneath the surface.

At the topline, revenues were flat as reported and down 3% on a currency-neutral basis. EPS, meanwhile, came in at $0.35/share. Both were above expectations. That’s a bit of good news to digest.

Gross margin, on the other hand, fell 40.2%, pressured heavily by tariffs and elevated markdown activity across channels.

At first glance, this looks uninspiring. But listening in to the earnings call, it was clear to me that much of this was likely intentional.

During the earnings call, for example, CEO Elliott Hill repeatedly emphasized the company’s “Win Now” actions, which include aggressively clearing aged and unhealthy inventory from classic footwear franchises. That decision alone created a roughly 5-point headwind to results.

In my view, NKE is willingly sacrificing near-term revenue to reset the marketplace, clean up discounting, and restore brand health.

There were also bright spots. NKE Running grew over 20% globally, wholesale in North America jumped 11%, and performance categories like football, training, and basketball showed signs of life.

This was offset, however, by Sportswear, China, and Converse, all of which remain early in the turnaround and continue to weigh on results.

The company also took a +$230M severance charge to right-size supply chain and technology costs. The charge is significant, but I believe it positions margins for recovery beginning in FY27.

Overall, this wasn’t a good quarter. It was a necessary one.

What Is The Outlook For NKE Stock?

If the market was looking for any reassurance, NKE’s guidance did the opposite.

From a transparency standpoint, NKE did make it very clear that the pressure we saw in Q3 is not ending anytime soon.

Looking ahead, the company expects revenue to be down low single digits through the end of the calendar year, with fiscal Q4 sales projected to fall 2% to 4%. That’s well below prior expectations for modest growth, and this is likely a key reason shares sold off so sharply.

The biggest drag continues to be Greater China, where Q4 revenue is expected to plunge roughly 20% as NKE intentionally reduces sell-in, cleans up the digital channel, and removes aged inventory from the marketplace.

These actions are expected to remain a headwind through fiscal 2027. At the same time, elevated tariffs will continue to pressure gross margins into the first quarter of fiscal 2027 before improvement begins in Q2.

From a more positive standpoint, North America is expected to post modest growth in Q4, wholesale momentum is building, and management expects gross margin expansion to begin later this year as promotional intensity eases and inventory health improves.

I believe the forward outlook is messy in the near term by design.

In my view, NKE is choosing to endure short-term pain now in order to reset the foundation for more profitable, sustainable growth ahead. Whether investors can actually buy into that story is another matter.

Is NKE Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

For me, this is where the opportunity becomes clear.

In my view, NKE is not missing because demand for the brand has permanently eroded. It is missing because the current management team is actively dismantling parts of the business that were propping up revenue in an unhealthy way. That’s a big distinction to me.

The company is choosing to take the pain now rather than let discounting, bloated inventory, and an overextended digital push continue to dilute the brand.

Investors, understandably, don’t like hearing that sales will decline through 2026 and that China will remain a drag well into fiscal 2027. Yes, that creates uncertainty, and uncertainty gets sold. But this is exactly the type of fear that has historically created attractive long-term entry points in market leaders.

At a sub-1.5x price-to-sales ratio, with wholesale momentum returning, performance categories growing, and margin recovery expected to begin next year, I see a company rebuilding its foundation while the market focuses only on the near-term mess.

Turnarounds rarely look pretty while they’re happening. In my view, NKE is in the uncomfortable spectrum of one. That said, for investors willing to look past the next few quarters, I view the recent pullback as a compelling buying opportunity rather than a reason to step aside.

data.title: Microsoft: Value Investor's Dream
data.description:
Thesis Summary

Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) is down a whopping 25% YTD and has even broken below its 200-week EMA.

I believe the stock is now a value investor's dream.

Indeed, this now squarely fits into the criteria once laid out by legendary investor Charlie Munger.

If all you ever did was buy high-quality stocks on the 200-week moving average, you would beat the S&P 500 by a large margin over time.

Source: Charlie Munger.

The only point of contention here would be whether Microsoft is still a quality company in the face of the AI threat.

I explain below why Microsoft remains one of the best companies to own worldwide, making this an incredible buying opportunity.

Microsoft Broke The 200-Week MA

Microsoft has now broken below the infamous 200-week EMA.

Even in the depths of the 2022 bear market, this level was respected, and yet here we are today.

While many would argue this is a bearish sign, and indeed, technical momentum is to the downside, the 200-week EMA should be seen as a reference point, not a make-or-break level.

Proof of this, for example, can be found below.

This study looks at the performance of the S&P 500 (SP500) after a break of the 200-day EMA, showing 1-year returns are actually well above the normal average.

Yes, the break of the 200-week EMA in MSFT isn’t bullish, but note that we also have strong volume support below at $320.

Ultimately, the question now is whether MSFT is a quality company now trading at a good price.

Is Microsoft Still A High Quality Stock?

What is quality when it comes to a business?

Obviously, growth and profitability are the first places to look, but more important are perhaps things like market dominance, moat, and pricing power.

Let’s break down Microsoft below.

Growth With Compounding Structure

Microsoft's revenue crossed $300 billion in 2025, growing at approximately 15% year-over-year.

In fact, MSFT has grown at over 14% over the last 5 years and even faster over the last decade.

More importantly, though, Microsoft has been achieving quality growth.

Azure is expanding revenues at nearly 40% annually.

Azure remains one of the three key players in a market that is accelerating. And on top of that, contracted capacity sits at over $625 billion, providing great visibility.

But the real magic is the fact that Azure has lifted operating margins towards nearly 50%.

This is not going anywhere, and neither is the core business.

The Moat: Deep, Layered, Self-Reinforcing

Microsoft's competitive moat is not a single point but rather multiple walls that create entrenched customers, switching costs, and network effects.

The Microsoft suite of products may not be the only game in town, but they continue to dominate enterprise software.

This report from 2024 shows Microsoft controlled almost one third of the market.

This makes switching costs high and creates inertia in their products.

Azure also provides architectural dependency.

Once your cloud infrastructure runs there, migration becomes a herculean task, and not one that most people would undergo without good reason.

And now the AI layer compounds this moat.

Microsoft has arguably been a leader in AI for some time, even if that leadership is fading.

The company, which owns a large part of OpenAI, was one of the first to introduce these tools to the Microsoft 365 suite of products.

For reference, GitHub Copilot has reached 4.7 million paid subscribers, which is a huge tailwind for Microsoft.

In my opinion, Microsoft is one of the few companies with the size, funds, and reach to actually create real AI penetration.

Risk: The AI Threat

So why is Microsoft selling off?

Beyond the recent geopolitical shock, Microsoft has also been caught up in the AI SaaSpocalypse narrative.

Three key risks remain going forward.

Firstly, AI does indeed commoditize all software. This seems far-fetched, and even in this case, Microsoft has something to gain through Azure.

Realistically, the issue here is that margins would get compressed, which is a real threat, but that’s not something we have seen yet.

Secondly, AI competition. Microsoft owns around 27% of OpenAI (OPENAI), and it does seem like the once clear leader has fallen behind. Both Google's Gemini and Anthropic (ANTHRO) are beginning to take the lead in terms of pure AI usage.

Thirdly, an AI recession could very much materialize, and again, that’s bad news for Microsoft, which has been investing heavily and also owns a large part of OpenAI.

We’ve already seen some scaling back in spending, and with the issues in the Middle East, this could actually tilt us into a recession.

This means all the leveraged spending on AI rollout is rolled back, and so are earnings estimates.

Final Thoughts

While I won’t deny that there’s a valid reason for the skepticism around Microsoft, this still looks like a great long-term opportunity.

Even in the worst-case scenario, it would take over a decade, in my opinion, to see Microsoft fully displaced.

In the meantime, Microsoft is printing money, compounding growth at 15% CAGR, and boasts one of the strongest balance sheets on Wall Street.

This is an overreaction, and in time, the market will begin to catch up, as will Microsoft.

data.title: Perpetua Resources: I See Upside As Stibnite Gains Momentum
data.description:
Thesis

Perpetua Resources Corp. (PPTA) is a mining company. But it isn’t actually mining or selling valuable materials yet. Instead, the company's still in the preparation stage, working on plans to build a major mining operation. Their main focus is a single project in Idaho called the Stibnite Gold Project. Essentially, they’ve found a lot of gold, plus something called antimony. Antimony is used in things like batteries, military equipment, and tech, so governments care about it a lot, especially because most of it comes from outside the U.S.

In terms of performance, PPTA's been crushing it, both short and longer term. Most recently, it hit my radar yesterday after it climbed over 10% after releasing its latest 10-K and announcing it lined up $2.7B in EXIM funding for its gold-antimony project. After getting up to speed, I’m leaning bullish here, as financing progress and strategic mineral exposure start to chip away at execution risk while leaving plenty of room for upside.

Perpetua Resources: Development-Stage Financial Overview

  • Operating loss (2025): $128.0M
  • Operating loss (2024): $52.1M
  • Exploration & pre-development (2025): $121.3M
  • Exploration & pre-development (2024): $45.3M.

Let's start off with the obvious, and that's that Perpetua is still a pre-revenue, single-asset developer. This quarter isn’t really about sales or margins, at least not in the usual mining sense. It’s about whether the management team can take their success in getting permits and turn it into a project that can actually be funded and built while still keeping control over costs, staying on schedule, and avoiding giving away too much ownership (dilution). I'd say that the most important update here is that the Export-Import Bank process has moved to the congressional notice stage. Even so, I wouldn’t go so far as to call it done.

  • Cash & cash equivalents (2025): $714.2M
  • Restricted cash (2025): $59.5M
  • Total cash incl. restricted: $773.7M
  • Net cash from financing (2025): $845.0M
  • Capital raised (2025): $862M.

The bigger operating loss doesn’t mean the mine is becoming less profitable or, for that matter, performing worse. Keep in mind, the company deliberately sped up early-stage work to get the project ready for construction after finishing the permitting phase. Engineering became the main area where money was being spent, with additional increases in costs for field support, legal work, sustainability efforts, and permitting. This matches what you’d expect when a company moves out of the planning and study phase and starts preparing to build: ordering equipment, bringing in contractors, and working on detailed designs. The problem, for me, isn’t that spending increased. The key question is whether that spending is actually reducing risks faster than it is creating the need for even more money. So yes, it looks more advanced and easier to finance, just more expensive and less flexible than it used to be.

  • Restricted cash (2025): $59.5M
  • Surety bond: $139.0M
  • Letters of credit total facility: $55.6M
  • IPCo contract total: $90.2M
  • ATCO contract total: $131.7M.

Liquidity looks better, largely because Perpetua sold a lot of shares and ended the year with plenty of cash. This matters because the company now has enough money on hand to cover early project work, detailed engineering, advance payments for equipment with long delivery times, and costs related to getting permits. All of it, notably, before project debt financing is actually secured.

  • Grant income (2025): $15.0M
  • Grant income (2024): $37.4M
  • Interest income (2025): $12.1M
  • Interest income (2024): $0.2M.

At the same time, a significant portion of it is already set aside for specific uses, such as restricted cash, letters of credit, surety support, vendor deposits, and other financial guarantees. In simple terms, the company’s financial position is stronger, but the cash isn’t freely available.

You'll also notice that income from grants became less useful because a previous government funding source ended. Nevertheless, interest income increased because money raised from equity was sitting in the company’s accounts and earning interest. It may make the non-operating part of the finances look better, but it’s not a reliable or long-term way of making money. Think of it as a short-term boost coming from financing, rather than real improvement in how profitable the business actually is. The true measure of success will come from how well future projects perform, not from this kind of income being reported right now.

Stepping back, why would the market even fund this story in the first place? The answer largely points to antimony, not exactly a common household name. Antimony is still massively important. But the United States doesn't produce much of it through mining at home. Meanwhile, China had previously limited its exports of antimony to the U.S., which made Western governments and industrial buyers a lot more concerned about having a reliable supply. I've discussed this on a few occasions on Seeking Alpha, notably in this piece. The point is, critical minerals still get funding, still get priority.

And it almost goes without saying that gold is another major factor supporting this. Even though prices were, let's call it, unstable back in March, just about everybody knows that gold still ended the quarter much higher than it was the year before. It’s basically what makes the economics look better on paper and keeps investors hooked on companies sitting on big, approved deposits.

PPTA: A Pre-Revenue Investment Case

Since the company hasn't started producing or earning revenue yet, its financials mainly show money being spent on engineering, early development work, getting permits, and so forth, with funding coming from selling equity. This means that the usual ways I evaluate companies in my reports and analyses, things like earnings multiples, historical P/E trends, FAST Graphs blended forecasts (which are simple, consistent, and usually quite accurate, so I rely on them a lot), P/S, EV/Sales, or CAGR, don’t really work well here and can end up giving a misleading impression.

I'm going to evaluate Perpetua by estimating what the project is worth today (net asset value per share), adjusted for risk, using the latest DCF model. According to the March 2026 technical report, the project has an after-tax net present value discounted at 5% (NPV5) of $3.457 billion, assuming a consensus gold price of $3,250. But with gold now trading close to $4,700, the model’s built-in sensitivity increases that value to about $6.46 billion, which works out to roughly $51.70 per share based on 125 million shares outstanding. Even after the EXIM notice, obviously, the stock's trading well below that updated value.

Again, I think this NAV method works best because, as I've already brought up several times to drive the point home, Perpetua isn’t generating any revenue yet. Its financials are mainly driven by spending on engineering, early construction work, and permitting, all funded through equity raises, which resulted in $714 million in cash at year-end.

So the DCF model clearly reflects the updated project economics: $2.524 billion in upfront capital costs, $767 million in ongoing (sustaining) costs, and operating costs of $29.32 per ton of material processed before credits. That noted, the mine's expected to run for 14.25 years and produce 4.195 million ounces of payable gold, along with 90.5 million pounds of antimony.

Before you buy, just note there are still several ways this “sustainable” story could fall apart. As of the latest update, the engineering work is only 45% complete, which definitely means capex could still rise beyond current expectations. At the same time, legal disputes and appeals over state permits could slow down the project timeline. Finally, no doubt, the gold price tailwind is real. But it can slip out of the spotlight as fast as it found its way into it.

Final Verdict

I'm initiating coverage of Perpetua Resources Corp. with a Buy. This isn't my usual type of investment, but I think that the overall risk–reward balance is very appealing. Even so, the chance for a pot of gold often comes with hazards, and there's a real downside if financing falls through, permits get delayed, or capex rises. Still, I like my odds here. If Stibnite gets funded and moves into construction, the bet starts to make sense, with the stock still sitting below its risk-adjusted value.

data.title: The Macro Implications Of Chipflation
data.description:
By Simon Wan and Tom Becker

AI hasn’t just increased demand for memory - it has fundamentally recast memory as a defining strategic asset in the AI era.

Micron Technology, Inc., March 18, 2026

Solid-state electronics control the modern economy. The critical nature of these microelectronics became front-page news during the COVID pandemic when supply chain-related chip shortages forced the shutdown of automotive production lines and doubled the secondary market prices for consumer goods like the PlayStation 5 console. Those acute 2020 supply shortages contributed to the surge in global goods price inflation and also catalyzed numerous fiscal expansions, like the US CHIPS ACT, as countries sought to create incentives to reshore microelectronic production and reduce supply chain dependencies. Whereas the 2020 jump in microelectronics prices was primarily attributable to supply chain disruptions, we now see a booming global industrial cycle generating excessive demand for these critical microelectronics. The current investment boom in artificial intelligence (AI) datacenters and remilitarization is generating a demand-driven increase in microchip prices.

Sharp rises in integrated circuit prices have the potential to generate another bout of global goods price inflation in 2026. In portfolios like the Tactical Opportunities Fund, inflation-related insights help to inform our directional short government bond positions. Higher chip prices also boost the international trade competitiveness of the developed market (DM) North Asian countries - Japan, South Korea, and Taiwan - that control the production of many of these critical microchips. The improvement in this region’s terms of trade helps to inform our cross-sectional longs to the stock and bond markets of these countries.

Integrated circuit prices defying Moore’s Law

The plots below show the recent price jumps for DRAM chips alongside the longer-term deflationary price dynamics. Prices for these critical but commoditized microelectronics have risen 17-fold over the last year, which is a sharp discontinuity from the multi-decade price declines. It is also worth noting that there had been a slowdown in exponential price declines even before the recent inflation: “The price of a gigabyte of DRAM has fallen by about a factor of ten every 5 years from 1957 to 2020. Since 2010, the price has fallen much more slowly, at a rate that would yield an order of magnitude over roughly 14 years.”1 Without Moore’s Law driving sustained price declines in commoditized microelectronics there will be upside risks to consumer goods prices in the coming years.

Cyclical DRAM price upswings defy a structurally deflationary backdrop

Recent commentary on microchip prices has tended to apply a supply-side framing that we view as misplaced. Instead, we see exponential price rises as a natural outcome of exponential demand. The chart below shows the analyst estimates for annual capital expenditures across the MAG 7 hyperscalers as a percentage of GDP. This is only a subset of the demand for microelectronics and doesn’t include the expanding national defense and consumer electronics demand. We believe that rising DRAM prices are rationally anticipating a price inelastic surge in microelectronics demand.

Mag 7 CapEx related to AI investment is causing demand-driven inflation

The global chips investment boom flows through DM Asia

Developed Asia is the production epicenter for the world’s most advanced microchips – centered around TSMC (TSM) in Taiwan. The region also controls the global production of less sophisticated but no-less-critical integrated circuits – with South Korea alone producing nearly 75% of the global DRAM chips. Those Dynamic Random Access Memory chips inspired the title of my favorite Daft Punk album but, more importantly, are a necessary component for all electronic devices. DRAM chips store data for rapid retrieval for AI data centers, military equipment, your iPhone, and any other good that touches the digital economy.

Terms-of-trade (TOT) is the ratio of a country’s export prices to its import prices. An improving ToT corresponds to a gain in international competitiveness and a rise in a country’s national income relative to its trading partners. The sources of ToT improvements are manifold – an appreciation of the exchange rate, shifts in product and commodity prices, or the deployment of new technologies. Typically associated with exchange rate appreciations, sustained ToT improvements make a country’s stocks and bonds attractive in the global cross-section as the rising national incomes support corporate earnings and debt sustainability.

The plot below helps to visualize how the price rises of high-tech goods has generated a sustained improvement in of DM Asia’s terms-of-trade relative to the large economies of the US, China, and the Eurozone. Despite having relatively weak currencies, Japan, South Korea, and Taiwan export goods – industrial goods like semiconductors, high tech machinery, and automobiles – that have been steadily rising in value in the global marketplace. Corporations in these countries have thus been able to grow margins and profits as their customers have been willing to ante up for the critical inputs fueling the simultaneous AI capex and defense spending booms. The governments in these countries have also benefitted from rising national incomes that have bolstered their debt sustainability dynamics.

Changes in ToT in the last few years have benefitted DM Asia economies

So what does it all mean for portfolios?

In our diversifying liquid alternative portfolios, like the BlackRock Tactical Opportunities Fund, we seek to deliver returns that are lowly correlated with stock and bond markets. We do so primarily by seeking out tactical relative value opportunities across countries’ stock, bond, and currency markets.

DM Asian equity markets have outperformed global equity indices over the last few years. We’ve been overweight in the region to varying degrees since 2020, and we’ve tactically rotated positioning across Japan, Taiwan, and South Korea. The high-tech exports of all three of these countries and sustained ToT improvements have translated into material earnings outperformance relative to other regions. On the back of recent price outperformance and positioning, we’ve trimmed regional exposures but continue to overweight Japanese equities.

In fixed income, we have increased government bond exposures across DM Asian issuers as rising national incomes boost debt sustainability metrics and overall borrowing capacity. On the back of late 2025 bond market sell-offs, we added cross-sectional longs in the portfolios in South Korea and Taiwan. In the last couple of months, we have added to directional short-duration positions based on inflation insights. We see upstream microelectronics price jumps translating into higher consumer goods prices in the coming quarters - an effect already visible in the computer software prices subcomponent of Consumer Price Index (CPI) that has posted a cumulative gain of 15.5% in the past 3 months.2

Source

1. Asya Bergal at AIIMPACTS summary of the longer-term price dynamics.

2. Computer software prices were up 6.5% month-on-month in the February CPI. This is a component with a stark weight difference between CPI vs PCE, with a weight of 0.03% in CPI and 1.1% in PCE. PCE is the Federal Reserve’s target variable for inflation and we see growing risks that PCE prices overshoot CPI based on weight and methodological differences.

This post originally appeared on BlackRock.

data.title: Constellation Brands - Looking At Cheap Consumer Products
data.description:
Some investors I follow fairly religiously to see what they do in the market - and when they make a move, I tend to pay attention. What I usually do when an investor I respect (a public one) makes a move, I tend to at the very least look at the company that they have elected to invest in, to see if it is something that I might want to invest in also. In those cases, I spend a lot of time on things like fundamentals, to make sure that I'm not buying something for reasons that might be good for an investor of that size (that I am following), but may be a sub-par choice for me.

Warren Buffett is one such investor. He not only bought stakes in Constellation Brands (STZ) in late 2024, which put the company on my radar, but he doubled down on the stake despite sector issues back in mid-2025. As far as official filings go, the ownership now amounts to about ~13M shares worth about $2B, which is a stake of over 7%, but below 8%.

This was of interest to me because Warren Buffett tends to look for very similar fundamentals as I do. Strong cash flows, good fundamentals, and a company undervalued by the market relative to what it is actually worth. A "bargain", in other words, or a "good company". I waited a bit longer than Warren Buffett to expand this stake, but expand it I did.

It wasn't a blind following from me, but rather confirmed a long-held belief that, first of all, the distillers, vintners, and alcohol companies of the world, after having seen a long period of overvaluation, are now undervalued. Secondly, that undervaluation is growing more and more, and growing more than I believe is justified by the overall decline in consumption by younger generations. First off, it's not yet clear whether this trend is just a small speedbump or actually something conservatively long-term.

Secondly, it completely underestimates this brand's ability to adapt.

And that, dear readers, is what I will look at here.

Constellation Brands - The upside echoes what's available in the market

That the market has had trouble is no secret. All you need to do is look at international giants like Diageo (DEO), Remy Cointreau (REMYF), or Pernod Ricard (PRNDY). I would further argue with you, dear readers, that this goes well beyond the traditional spirit brands and has "leaked over" into the valuations for things like beer and lighter spirits as well, though these are somewhat different sub-brands.

My initial investment in Constellation Brands is now at a negative RoR. I bought a bit too early. As usual, though, this doesn't really bother me for the long term. My latest addition, which I made in the fall of 2025, not that long after Buffett's, is up, because the company has somewhat recovered. Of course, this is in no way a "victory" so far.

So let's look at this company and why I believe it will outperform.

First, I believe the company is extremely well-managed. This is not only clear from Buffett's investment, but from how the forecast accuracy "flows". This company never misses estimates. The company gives conservative numbers - as highlighted by its tendency to either hit or beat such estimates, with any margin of error that makes sense here.

Combine this with an IG-grade credit rating, and you have two solid indicators for quality. We then turn to the company's balance sheet for a qualitative analysis of the numbers and the filings. It's not "clean", but the worries therein are hardly enough to send me running for the hills, so to speak.

The main problem, I would say, is the considerable net debt that the company has taken on. This is also implied in the debt/equity ratio, which is above 55%. Net debt for the company comes to a level of $10.5B, and this means that the company's total equity is actually lower than its liabilities.

So, what is the reason for these significant debt levels?

The company has, relatively recently, been through a spending and investing spree for its LATAM operations, the Mexican beer, as well as some M&A's. It's these investments, alongside the expansion operations within them, that are the reason for what's happening here. STZ is funding most of this with debt, not equity. Further, and this is something I don't like about the company, which causes me to discount it a bit, is that the company seems to use debt for buybacks of shares. You can see this in the cash flow statement, where, for some time, buybacks have been funded by more capital than is available from cash from operations.

I cannot argue against the valuation rationale. At this price, STZ is de facto undervalued, which means that the company is technically and efficiently taking advantage of the low operations. It's also an impressive "FCF printer".

Unfortunately, this doesn't change the fact that this is done with debt, and this is always a negative in my book. The current net debt is not unmanageable. If STZ was to aim its considerable "cash cannon" strictly at the company's debt, it would take less than 6 years to become debt-free - but this is unlikely to be done. While EBIT interest coverage remains strong, the leverage is one of the primary reasons that I mark this company down.

Some have suggested that STZ could issue shares and equity to dial this down - I view the notion of issuing shares at this valuation to be a foolish one, given that they would be giving away parts of the company at too low a price. Dilution is something that I view as attractive at a high price, not a low one.

STZ has a broken fiscal, meaning we go the 3Q26 results in early January of 2026. Those are the results we'll focus on here. As an operational and financial backdrop to these results, the company is, as I mentioned, going through a very challenging time. The challenging time is represented by significant AEPS drops. In 2026, we're seeing a decline of double digits, and the company is expecting only very slow growth back up from these numbers - even as far out as 2029E, I view it unlikely that earnings will have recovered back to 2025A levels.

The reason for this is substandard growth in beer. There has been a slump in beer volume growth by LATAM consumers. When we combine this with higher leverage from aforementioned factors, not to mention static input costs inflation, tariffs, and an overall weaker wine segment as well, it comes to a formula and result that cannot be expected to be positive.

The pressure on the stock, from this perspective, is justified - to some extent. It has also been added to by the company divesting sales volume - selling some low-performing brands, which nevertheless add to the pressure here.

Where I believe the market is getting it wrong, though, is that it seems to think that this will cause fundamental deterioration. It has not, nor will it (in my view). The company still maintains margins of over 39%, and the overall numbers, as well as the recent results, imply more of a temporary slump than a total deterioration. This is confirmed by the somewhat mixed growth results in the 3Q26.

The company is combating market declines (volume) with slight price adjustments, while in Wine, they are lowering prices against a slight growth in volume.

Overall, these results do not support the notion of a continued fundamental deterioration in sales, though obviously an eye needs to be kept on them. The very slight adjustments in COGS and marketing also suggest the overall pressures are, for the time being, over. I believe, based on these numbers and trends, that the potential for further declines are overstated.

As you can see, much of the background as to why the company is down has in fact not to do much with even "less sales", but rather with financial-strategic adjustment, such as interest, debt, and sales of operations.

I further argue that this also echoes what else is being said in other parts of the industry. Diageo is not reporting more significant slumps in its sales or earnings. They are instead reporting a lack of growth back up.

That a lack of growth or reversal is an issue is not something we need to debate. But the debate becomes, if we want to be conservative, what valuation should a rebased Diageo, or in this case, Constellation Brands, trade at?

Here are my thoughts on this.

Constellation Brands valuation - Upside at any conservative multiple

The best-quality alcohol and liquor companies tended, historically, to trade between 18-30x P/E. This is an assumption that, with the current worries, requires reconsideration. So let's reconsider it.

Regardless of how you calculate these things, alcohol is something that many people will continue to consume. The slight hurdles in sales are not evidence of any material change in behaviour, in my view - it's a slump, and a slight one. The reason that the companies are making less money is as much related to financial considerations and balance sheet management as they are to operational strategic considerations.

STZ, which has a historical average of 18-21, is currently trading below 12.9x. This is despite a 9.8% AEPS growth until 2029 which, as I mentioned early on, is as safe a forecast as we can find. Given the company's traditions, we would not be wrong to expect, in fact, outperformance.

I will rebase the company, but I will do so at a P/E range of 15-17x, with an average of 16x, which I believe to be a very conservative fair value assumption. Going by a 2029E growth back to $15.3/share, which I remind you is not even where the company was before, would deliver an annualized capital appreciation of about 19.1%, and with the 2.7% dividend (not exactly high, but good enough here, to a total of 21.3% per year.

And this is what I consider a very conservative assumption. Using more historically accurate P/E ratchets this up to 25-30% per year very quickly. But even disregarding those, the company would have to drop below a forward P/E of 10x, before you'd start losing money at this price.

So, under current assumptions, the company that is expected to grow earnings by 9-10% per year, would have to trade 30% lower than today, to generate losses annualized for you - or stop growing in accordance with forecasts, which, based on the company's historical accuracy and recent forecasts I consider to be unlikely.

I therefore say that the company is undervalued. I work with a 16x P/E average forecast, which comes to a PT, updated for 2026, of $165/share.

I therefore say the upside here to be good despite everything, and consider the company to be a "BUY".

Thesis

Constellation Brands is in some geographies a leading beer and wine business that's been going through a significant slump due to sales declines. However, I believe the market is underestimating the upside and overestimating market risk. Based on the company's estimate accuracy, fundamentals, and yield, I would put a $165/share price target to clear my hurdle of 15% annualized, which the company is now doing.

I would, based on the current company share price and estimates, give the company a "BUY" rating. Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (in italics).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 4/5 "stars" here, which is enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this, and a price target of $165/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Buy" rating as of April 2026.

data.title: A Look At BlackBerry's Growth Drivers, Ahead Of Fourth-Quarter Earnings Report
data.description:
Last December, BlackBerry Limited (BB) shares fell by 10% after posting third-quarter results. Since then, the stock returned -14.85%, underperforming the S&P 500 (SPY), which fell by 4.91%. Last month, the seven-day sessions reasserted the bearish sentiment, hurting investors. With BB stock still in a downtrend, what will the fourth-quarter earnings report look like?

Fourth-Quarter Earnings Expectations

Readers may view the earnings expectations from this tab. Seven analysts forecast an EPS of $0.15 in the fiscal period ending in February 2026. That is a nearly six-fold increase in Y/Y growth on a forward price-to-earnings of 21.6 times.

Fewer analysts offered an EPS forecast for FY 2028. Four analysts forecast a $0.19 EPS in two years for a forward P/E of 16.4 times. Comparing BlackBerry’s stock valuation to its peers, the stock scored a B on valuation. Its Growth grade is comparatively better than that of two other peers: On Semiconductor (ON) and Kyndryl Holdings (KD).

Above: BB stock ranks 25th out of 43 in the industry. Rubrik (RBRK) ranks higher.

Below: Kyndryl shares lost half their value in 2026, undermining its B+ profitability grade and underscoring its A- on valuation. On February 9, Kyndryl delayed key filings that included the 10-Q. It is reviewing its accounting. Additionally, it lost its Chief Financial Officer and General Counsel. The senior vice president, Vineet Khurana, stepped down from his position.

Opportunities With BlackBerry

BlackBerry touted QNX’s total addressable market in the automotive sector. BMW (BMWKY), which is a champion in implementing innovative OEM, is embracing QNX. It demands performance and intelligence in the system. That is why it will implement BlackBerry’s operating system and hypervisor software solution. That should encourage BMW’s competitors, like Mercedes-Benz Group (MBGAF), to continue using QNX as the backbone in their software-defined vehicles. Volkswagen (VWAGY) used QNX in its infotainment systems more than a decade ago. However, it formed a joint venture with Rivian (RIVN) worth $6 billion last year.

When Rivian launches the R2 SUV this year, followed by a smaller Rivian R3 in 2027, both models will use a new electronic control unit architecture. Investors are growing increasingly bullish on Rivian’s prospects. Despite a short float of nearly 18%, the stock has trended higher since early February.

Above: Compare Volkswagen’s grades to those of Mercedes-Benz and BMW. They all score an A- or higher on valuation. VW stock ranks fourth in the industry. Ferrari (RACE) offers strong profitability and growth, but shares trade at a premium. Still, analysts are bullish on RACE stock, where the EPS revisions grade is a B+.

BlackBerry Design Wins

BlackBerry added General Embedded Market (“GEM”) designs at a faster rate than historically. If the company brings the GEM into production faster than expected, it would accelerate its growth in the automotive market.

The SDP 8.0 release is still a potential revenue generator. Automotive accounted for 80% of demand. Moreover, GEM is growing quickly, and BlackBerry believes that this will accelerate the backlog growth. In the third quarter, GEM counted on many leading industrial automation companies adopting SDP 8.0. NASA also added SDP 8.0 to its supported operating systems.

Above: QNX posted record quarterly revenue in the last quarter. That resulted in revenue of $68.7 million, at the top end of its guidance range and up by 10% Y/Y.

Secure Communications is still a meaningful revenue generator for BlackBerry. The unit posted revenue of $67 million. It benefited from lower churn in UEM. Additionally, it benefited from traction with the U.S. government. The company needs to continue increasing customer satisfaction rates. That would lead to falling churn, lifting gross margin. The adjusted gross margin for Secure Communications was 72% in the third quarter.

Q4 And Full-Year 2026 Outlook

BlackBerry expects most of its revenue of up to $148 million in the fourth quarter will come from QNX revenue ($71 million to $77 million). Secure Communications is still a big portion of total revenue:

The firm is expecting cash from operations for the quarter and the year.

Your Takeaway

Investors gave up on BlackBerry since last October, when shares traded at above $5.00. Selling accelerated after the firm posted its third-quarter report. At $3.24, speculators do not have much to lose.

Investors considering BB stock should monitor the increasingly bearish conditions for the automotive sector. A global recession, led by the ongoing shipment pause in the Strait of Hormuz, would hurt BlackBerry’s automotive revenue prospects.

Expect quarterly results to meet expectations. Therefore, BlackBerry shares retain a ‘buy’ rating ahead of its earnings report.

data.title: Marvell: Nvidia Deal Supercharges An Undervalued AI Leader
data.description:
I Remain Bullish On MRVL

Even before the latest news around Nvidia's (NVDA) investment announcement, Marvell Technology, Inc. (MRVL) stock was one of the best-performing major semiconductor stocks on this turbulent market (on a YTD basis), as can be seen from the YCharts data below:

I was bullish on MRVL since March 2025, and my most recent bullish update on it came out in late January 2026 - I liked that MRVL's custom silicon and electro-optics businesses were projected to double by 2028, and the very fact that their data center revenue was exploding, which seemed to provide better earnings visibility and promised some bullish revisions for forward EPS and sales estimates. Now, Marvell is clearly moving to a specialized AI infrastructure powerhouse with its custom ASICs, optical interconnects, PCIe retimers, and Ethernet switches, and the $2 billion investment from Nvidia has just validated its tech moat. My previously laid out bullish points still hold, and I think the stock remains undervalued because its forward earnings consensus estimates are likely underestimated to date.

Why Do I Think So?

MRVL's fiscal 2026 sales of $8.195 billion were up by 42% on a YoY basis, with the main growth driver being data center revenue, which went up by 46% YoY and surpassed $6 billion in size. In Q4, the DC revenues were up 9% QoQ (+21% YoY) with a 74% share in the total mix - Marvell is gradually increasing this share quarter after quarter, which is good to see because thanks to this shift, the firm's EBITDA margins have been steadily expanding:

Just a few years ago, the company's custom silicon business was generating virtually zero in sales, and in fiscal 2026, it brought in ~$1.5 billion, effectively doubling as the programs entered full production. Marvell has managed to exit FY2026 with record design wins, seeing its bookings accelerating at a historic pace. The management guided for Q1 revenues of $2.4 billion (27% YoY) when the consensus was $2.28 billion. If we scroll back a little bit, we'll see that in September 2025, the management team was seeing $9.5 billion in FY2027 sales. Then by December, they adjusted this target to $10 billion, and in March 2026, they aggressively raised this forecast to effectively approach $11 billion. So, I guess all these updates were a strong reason why MRVL remained steady when others fell against the backdrop of the Middle East tensions.

MRVL's data center revenues are projected to grow by 40% YoY in FY2027, and the interconnect segment should add 50% in sales on a YoY basis. Plus, they're going to exit FY2027 at a run rate of >$3 billion in terms of quarterly revenues - the growth is clearly not going anywhere when we think of calendar 2027-2028, in my understanding.

As we begin fiscal 2027, we are seeing very strong demand across our entire data center portfolio with bookings accelerating at a record pace. This robust demand is reflected in our guidance for the first quarter of fiscal 2027, the total company revenue forecasted now to grow 8% sequentially at the midpoint to $2.4 billion. Looking ahead, we expect to grow revenue every quarter this fiscal year at a similarly strong sequential rate, which would result in Q4 revenue exceeding $3 billion exiting this year.

Source: MRVL's Q4 2026 earnings call.

Most analysts agree with me as they've already raised their FY2030-2035 revenue estimates by 10-25% over the past month, according to Seeking Alpha.

I believe even after this amount of bullish revisions, analysts might not be fully capturing MRVL's post-2028 growth prospects.

They acquired Celestial AI and XConn Technologies in early 2026, and these 2 purchases should help Marvell widen its TAM as they scale. The thing is that historically, Marvell was known for just connecting separate racks and clusters, being a major player in the "scale-out" networking market. But with Celestial AI and XConn, they're capturing the "scale-up" market as well, which is about the ultra-high-speed connectivity within a single server rack between GPUs/XPUs. It's a new revenue venue, which is hard to quantify right now, and I believe it's not yet priced in by the majority of analysts who are still waiting for material contributions (i.e., from Celestial and XConn).

With XConn, Marvell has already launched the Structera S 30260 and S 60260 switches to target AI memory pooling and disaggregation so that hyperscalers can basically re-use older DRAM with new XPUs. With Celestial, Marvell has gotten a new photonic fabric tech that enables CPO (co-packaged optics), bypassing the current copper bottlenecks. According to SNS Insider's research, the CPO market is going to reach over $124 billion in TAM by 2032, and if so, it's going to be a massive addition to MRVL's total addressable market.

In addition to the above TAM enlargement prospects, we have to discuss the recent Nvidia's investment as it's the main topic now.

The $2 billion investment came along with a new MRVL/NVDA partnership for integrating Marvell into the Nvidia AI factory through NVLink Fusion.

Marvell will provide custom AI accelerators, or XPUs, and NVLink Fusion-compatible scale-up networking, while Nvidia will offer up its Vera CPU, ConnectX NICs, Bluefield DPUs, NVLink interconnect and Spectrum-X switches, and rack-scale AI compute, the companies said in a statement.

In addition, the two companies will work together on telecom networks and bring artificial intelligence infrastructure to them, offering Nvidia Aerial AI-RAN for 5G and 6G, and advanced world-class networking for AI.

Source: Seeking Alpha News.

If we think about the potential implications for MRVL, we'll see some interesting things. Historically, the connectivity space was like a battlefield between proprietary standards, between Nvidia's NVLink/InfiniBand and open standards like Ultra Ethernet/UALink backed by Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), Broadcom (AVGO), and Marvell. So, with this new partnership, Marvell can now integrate into NVLink Fusion and provide custom XPUs and scale-up networking that is fully and natively compatible with Nvidia's ecosystem - the firm's R&D spend is not largely de-risked, and the serviceable market has expanded overnight, in my understanding. Plus, the Celestial AI tech can now move to monetization at a faster pace because Nvidia and Marvell will collaborate deeply on silicon photonics - this is exactly what gives me higher conviction that current revenue and EPS estimates are still being underestimated.

The firm was expecting Celestial AI to begin contributing in the second half of fiscal 2028, with an ARR of $500 million by Q4 FY2028, but the new partnership can accelerate the timeline and let the management team update the projection once again (just like they did multiple times in terms of the consolidated sales guidance).

Even if we don't add much to the current FY2028 EPS consensus figure, and say that there will be only a 5% addition (based on the latest beats and the Nvidia news), I think MRVL should now have a fully-justified price-to-earnings multiple of above 20x. I'll take the range of 20-25x on FY2028 earnings for simplicity. With that in mind, I get the following output for my simple valuation model:

22.5x * ($5.44 * 1.05)= $128.52/share

From that, as you can see, the upside potential should be close to 30% in the next 12-16 months. It doesn't look like something impossible - MRVL had a similar price tag back in late 2024. Now, they have more justifications to come back to it:

Final Words

Marvell might feel massive market pressure if the management's optimism doesn't age as expected, and we see some delays for some of their projects. We shouldn't cancel out the competition with AVGO, particularly in custom ASICs, where Marvell's peer can take a large market share and play with pricing (hence eroding MRVL's margins). The margin contraction risk is real when we take into account the rise in ASIC programs, which generally carry lower gross margins compared to their off-the-shelf merchant silicon. And it's already happening - despite EBITDA margin rising on a TTM basis, the Q4 gross margin ticked down to 59% compared to 60.1% last year.

Although the risks look real, I think Marvell is on the right path to capturing more market shares in optical DSPs, custom ASICs, and XPU attach infrastructure, hence becoming a key partner for mega-cap semis players like Nvidia. I think the current consensus estimates, despite being already adjusted higher over the past few months, are still not fully pricing in the firm's earnings growth prospects. I see an undervaluation of about 30% to the current stock price, so I keep my Buy rating in place for now.

Thank you for reading!

data.title: Celsius' Growing Dollar Share At Compelling Valuations - Dip Buying Opportunity
data.description:
I previously covered Celsius Holdings, Inc. (NASDAQ:CELH) in December 2025, discussing why the stock was an even better Buy after the prior market over-reaction to the upcoming inventory transition in FQ4'25.

This was because the Alani Nu acquisition had already triggered the expanding energy drink dollar share and the potentially outsized growth prospects from FQ1'26/FY2026 onwards, aided by the compelling FY2026 valuations and the healthy balance sheet.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am reiterating my Buy rating for the CELH stock, given the stock's notably oversold position during the market-wide pessimism surrounding the uncertain macroeconomic environment and the intensifying market competition.

My optimism is attributed to their expanding energy drink dollar share at the expense of its direct competitors (aided by the Alani Nu and Rockstar acquisitions), the H2'26 volume tailwinds from the ongoing integration into PepsiCo's (PEP) distribution system, and the compelling valuations after the recent correction.

CELH Faces Macro/Competition Headwinds

Since my last Buy rating, the bears have proven my bullish thesis wrong indeed, with CELH already losing -22% of its value against the wider market at -7% and a similar correction also observed in its beverage and Consumer Packaged Goods [CPG] peers in varying degrees.

1. Uncertain Macro & Intensifying Competition

Part of their headwinds are attributed to the increasingly uncertain macroeconomic outlook, with the higher inflationary pressure and the diverging spending trends from the K shaped economy likely to trigger further market pessimism surrounding the CPG sector.

This is especially since the higher fuel prices from the ongoing Iran conflict, currently at $4.01 per gallon as of March 31, 2026 (compared to $2.896 per gallon in December 2025 and $2.61 per gallon in 2019), may potentially trigger reduced discretionary spending trends through the "convenience and gas station retail channels."

This is worsened by the intensifying market competition from private label players, most recently from Costco (COST), attributed to the latter's launch of 24-pack Kirkland Signature Sparkling Energy Drink with three flavor varieties/low calories at an estimated selling price of $0.70 per 12-ounce cans.

When compared to CELH's singular 12-ounce can pricing of $2.28 and bulk 12-ounce can pricing of $1.49 (through the 12-pack), I can understand why the market has decided to de-risk first and ask questions later.

The same has been observed in CELH's stock price correction by -49.3% from the 52 week highs, given the potential shift in consumer buying trends to the cheaper, private label offerings.

2. Dollar Share Gains

Otherwise, I am of the opinion that CELH's selloff has been unwarranted albeit triggering a great dip buying opportunity, attributed to their ability to drive the expanding energy MULO+ w/c dollar share of 19.9% in Q4'25 (+9 points YoY), aided by the Alani Nu and Rockstar acquisitions.

This is especially since their dollar share gains have occurred at the expense of its two direct competitors, Monster Beverage (MNST) at 33.9% in Q4'25 (-0.5 points YoY) and Red Bull at 34.8% (-2.7 points YoY).

Readers must also note that CELH's core offering, CELSIUS® brand, has reported a notable revenue acceleration by +7.5% YoY in FY2025, after the trough +3% YoY observed in FY2024, albeit after the deceleration from the 5Y CAGR of +78.3% (between FY2019 and FY2024, pre Alani Nu and Rockstar acquisitions).

I am of the opinion that there are further growth tailwinds entering FY2026, attributed to the ongoing Alani Nu integration into PEP's distribution system in Q1'26 and Rockstar by Q2'26, with it implying further growth opportunities in H2'26.

The same has been hinted by the management, with the ongoing integration into PEP's distribution system already driving a pro forma Alani Nu revenue growth by +136% YoY in FQ4'25.

Beyond acquisitions, CELH has also highlighted their plans to intensify their product innovation and limited time offer cadence, both of which are likely to "bringing in new guests while increasing the frequency of the existing base" and "maintaining elevated spend and frequency levels throughout the year," similar to Chipotle Mexican Grill's (CMG) renewal strategy.

This reason may also be why CELH has already launched the new Fizz-Free line nationally, with it expected to appeal to new target groups "that prefer beverages without carbonation or like the optionality of fizz or fizz-free."

Combined with the projected multi-year gross margin recovery from FQ4'25 levels of 47.4% to the management target of "low 50s," I am of the opinion that the management remains well positioned to drive a robust, multi-year profitable growth trend ahead.

3. Compellingly Valued, Profitable Growth Trend

Therefore, while CELH has yet to offer any forward guidance, it is unsurprising that the consensus have raised their forward estimates, with the energy drink company expected to report a robust profitable growth from the annualized FQ4'25 levels (attributed to the accretion of Alani Nu from Q2'25 and Rockstar Energy from Q4'25) to the consensus FY2028 estimates.

This is with a top/bottom-line (GAAP EPS) growth estimates at a CAGR of +12.4%/+144.5% through FY2028, with it building upon the historical top-line growth profile at a 5Y CAGR of +80.7% - with it apparent that the recent selloff has been mostly sentiment driven instead of fundamentals.

As a result of the -49.3% meltdown from the 52 week highs and the raised consensus forward estimates (including the accretive impact of Alani Nu and Rockstar Energy acquisitions), I am of the opinion that CELH is rather compelling at FWD EV/EBITDA valuations of 13.86x and FWD P/E GAAP valuations of 22.06x.

Based on the FWD P/E GAAP valuations of 22.06x and the consensus EPS growth estimates at a 3Y CAGR of +110.7%, CELH is even cheaper at my estimated 3Y PEG GAAP ratio of 0.19x, compared to the sector median of 1.07x.

When comparing CELH's FWD EV/EBITDA valuations of 13.86x, FY2025 adj EBITDA performance of 24.6%, and my estimated 3Y PEG GAAP ratio of 0.19x to its peers, including:

  • beverage/snacks - PEP at 12.96x/15.9% in FY2025/1.17x,
  • beverage - Keurig Dr Pepper (KDP) at 8.59x/29.2% in FY2025/0.60x,
  • beverage - MNST at 23.14x/32.4% in FY2025/2.24x, and
  • beverage - Coca-Cola Company (KO) at 20.66x/33% in FY2025/3.67x, respectively,

I am of the opinion that CELH's profitable growth trends are reasonably valued here, significantly aided by the extremely healthy balance sheet at a net debt to adj EBITDA ratio of 0.43x in FY2025 despite the recent Alani Nu acquisition.

This is compared to its direct peers, including PEP at 2.65x, KDP at 0.42x, MNST at 1.02x, and KO at 1.97x.

This development implies that CELH remains well capitalized to drive renewed growth through further product innovation and/or accretive acquisitions, all of which underscoring its compellingly valued, profitable growth investment thesis after the recent selloff.

CELH Remains A Buy For The Contrarian

For now, CELH has already lost much of its 2025/2026 gains, with the stock currently retesting the $33s support levels by the time of writing and it remaining to be seen when the bulls may defend a trading floor.

Otherwise, thanks to the ongoing selloff, there is now an expanded upside potential of +52.6% to my long-term price target of $51.60, based on the FWD P/E GAAP valuations of 22.06x (near to its peer group P/E mean of ~21.85x) and the consensus FY2028 GAAP EPS estimates of $2.34.

Combined with the H2'26 tailwinds from the integration of Alani Nu/Rockstar acquisitions, the multi-year gross margin recovery prospects, and the ongoing cancellation of its dilutive SBC expenses through share repurchases, I am cautiously reiterating my Buy rating for the CELH stock.

This is albeit with the caveat that CELH is only suitable for those with a higher risk appetite and a longer investing trajectory, given:

  • the breach against the November 2025 bottom of $37s,
  • the stock's still elevated short interest ratio of 7.97% despite the ongoing meltdown/oversold technical indicators,
  • the notably pessimistic stock market sentiments, and
  • the lack of forward guidance pending further clarity on their FY2026 growth prospects.
